scenario_title: Add RR to CNAME owner while deleting the CNAME in same UPDATE
test_rationale: This scenario is the smallest wire image that exercises the sequencing gap: the owner starts with only a CNAME, then the same UPDATE removes that CNAME and tries to add an A at the same name. Because RFC 2136 does not define whether the server must evaluate CNAME exclusivity before or after applying the delete_rr, different implementations may either accept the transition and publish the A or reject it and preserve the CNAME. The follow-up queries make the final state observable even when the reply code alone is not sufficient.

group_1.behavior: Evaluates the UPDATE against the post-delete state, accepts the name transition, and ends with no CNAME and one A RR at host.campus.edu.
group_1.post_state: CNAME absent; A rrset present at host.campus.edu.

group_2.behavior: Evaluates CNAME exclusivity against the pre-state or rejects the mixed transition, leaving the original CNAME in place and not installing the A RR.
group_2.post_state: CNAME remains present; A rrset absent at host.campus.edu.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2136 §3.4 and §7.2 define UPDATE prerequisites and the update section as an unordered set of changes, but do not specify an intra-message ordering rule for a delete_rr followed by an add_rr at the same owner. RFC 2181 §10.1 makes CNAME exclusivity clear, yet leaves unspecified whether that constraint is checked against the pre-update state or after the CNAME deletion within the same UPDATE. As a result, compliant servers can diverge on both the reply RCODE and the resulting rrsets.
